content:
# Acts Chapter 27 -- The EMET Bible (Literal)
**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus, PDF pages 259-260 | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/ACT_027_page_001.jpg, ACT_027_page_002.jpg

---

**1.** And when it was decided [for] us to sail-away into Italy, they were handing-over both Paul and certain other prisoners to a centurion, Julius by name, of [the] Augustan cohort.

**2.** And having embarked on a ship of Adramyttium, being-about to sail to the places along Asia, we put-to-sea, Aristarchus, a Macedonian of Thessalonica, being with us.

**3.** And on the next [day] we were brought-down to Sidon; and Julius, having treated Paul with-human-kindness, permitted [him] to go to [his] friends [and] to obtain care.

**4.** And from-there having put-to-sea, we sailed-under [the lee of] Cyprus, because the winds were contrary.

**5.** And having sailed-through the sea along Cilicia and Pamphylia, we came-down to Myra of Lycia.

**6.** And there the centurion, having found a ship of Alexandria sailing into Italy, put us into it.

**7.** And sailing-slowly in a considerable [number] of days, and with-difficulty having come off Cnidus, the wind not permitting us [further], we sailed-under [the lee of] Crete off Salmone;

**8.** and with-difficulty coasting-along it, we came to a certain place called Fair Havens, near to which was [the] city of Lasea.

**9.** And considerable time having passed, and the voyage being already dangerous because even the Fast had already gone-by, Paul was admonishing [them],

**10.** saying to them: Men, I perceive that with injury and much loss, not only of the cargo and of the ship but also of our souls, the voyage is about to be.

**11.** But the centurion was being-persuaded by the pilot and by the ship-owner rather than by the [things] being spoken by Paul.

**12.** And the harbor being unsuitable for wintering, the majority gave counsel to put-to-sea from-there, if somehow they might be-able, having arrived at Phoenix, a harbor of Crete looking toward [the] southwest and toward [the] northwest, to winter there.

**13.** And [the] south-wind having blown gently, having supposed [they had] obtained the purpose, having weighed [anchor], they were sailing-along Crete close-by.

**14.** But after not much [time], a tempestuous wind called Euraquilo [the northeaster] struck down from it;

**15.** and the ship having been seized and not being-able to face the wind, having given [way], we were being carried-along.

**16.** And running-under [the lee of] a certain small-island called Cauda, we were-able with-difficulty to become masters of the skiff;

**17.** which having hoisted-up, they were using helps, undergirding the ship; and fearing lest into the Syrtis they should fall, having lowered the gear, thus they were being carried-along.

**18.** And we being violently storm-tossed, on the next [day] they were making a jettison;

**19.** and on the third [day] with-their-own-hands they threw-out the tackle of the ship.

**20.** And neither sun nor stars appearing for many days, and no small storm lying-upon [us], all hope of our being-saved was henceforth being taken-away.

**21.** And much lack-of-appetite existing, then Paul, having stood in [the] midst of them, said: O men, it was necessary indeed, having obeyed me, not to have put-to-sea from Crete and to have gained this injury and [the] loss.

**22.** And now I urge you to be-of-good-cheer, for [there] will be no casting-away of soul from among you, but only of the ship.

**23.** For there stood-by me this night an angel of the God whose I am and whom I serve,

**24.** saying: Do not fear, Paul; before Caesar you must stand; and behold, God has granted to you all those sailing with you.

**25.** Therefore be-of-good-cheer, men; for I believe God that thus it will be according to the manner it has been spoken to me.

**26.** But upon a certain island we must fall.

**27.** And when the fourteenth night came, [as] we were being carried-about in the Adriatic, toward [the] middle of the night the sailors were suspecting some land to be approaching them.

**28.** And having sounded, they found twenty fathoms; and having gone-forward a little and again having sounded, they found fifteen fathoms.

**29.** And fearing lest somewhere against rough places we should fall, having cast four anchors from [the] stern, they were praying [for] day to come.

**30.** And the sailors seeking to flee from the ship, and having lowered the skiff into the sea under pretense as [though] from [the] prow being-about to extend anchors,

**31.** Paul said to the centurion and to the soldiers: Unless these remain in the ship, you are not able to be saved.

**32.** Then the soldiers cut-away the ropes of the skiff and let it fall-away.

**33.** And until [the] day was about to come, Paul was encouraging all to partake of food, saying: Today [is the] fourteenth day [that] watching you continue without-food, having taken nothing.

**34.** Therefore I encourage you to partake of food, for this is for your deliverance; for not a hair from the head of any of you will perish.

**35.** And having said these [things] and having taken bread, he gave-thanks to God before all, and having broken [it], he began to eat.

**36.** And all having become of-good-cheer, they also themselves took food.

**37.** And we were, all the souls in the ship, two hundred seventy-six.

**38.** And having been satisfied with food, they were lightening the ship, throwing-out the wheat into the sea.

**39.** And when day came, they were not recognizing the land; but a certain bay they were observing having a beach, into which they were planning, if they might be-able, to drive-out the ship.

**40.** And having cast-off the anchors, they left [them] in the sea, at-the-same-time having loosened the bands of the rudders; and having hoisted the foresail to the [wind] blowing, they were holding [course] toward the beach.

**41.** But having fallen-upon a place between-two-seas, they ran-aground the vessel; and the prow having stuck-fast remained unmoved, but the stern was being broken-up by the force of the waves.

**42.** And [the] plan of the soldiers was that they should kill the prisoners, lest anyone having swum-out should escape.

**43.** But the centurion, wishing to save Paul, prevented them from [the] plan, and commanded those being-able to swim, having thrown-themselves-off first, to get upon the land;

**44.** and the rest, some indeed upon planks, and some upon some [things] from the ship. And thus it came-about [that] all were brought-safely through upon the land.

---

## EMET's Textual Notes

**Manuscript and Folio:** Codex Sinaiticus (aleph), 4th century CE. The text of Acts 27 spans two columns on page 259 (folio q77 verso, columns 1-4, covering vv. 1-27) and continues on page 260 (folio q78 recto, columns 1-2, covering vv. 28-44, with Acts 28 beginning in column 3).

**Verse 1 -- "it was decided" (ekrithe):** The aorist passive of krino. Sinaiticus reads ekrithe, consistent with the Alexandrian text. The "we" passage resumes here (first person plural), indicating Luke's presence.

**Verse 2 -- Adramyttium:** Sinaiticus spells this Adramyttenoi (dative), an adjectival form. The city was a port in Mysia, northwest Asia Minor.

**Verse 5 -- "Myra of Lycia":** Sinaiticus reads Myra (accusative plural neuter). Some later manuscripts read "Lystra" by scribal confusion; Sinaiticus preserves the correct reading.

**Verse 8 -- "Fair Havens" (Kalous Limenas):** A place name transliterated from Greek. Still identifiable on the southern coast of Crete today.

**Verse 9 -- "the Fast" (ten nesteian):** Refers to Yom Kippur (the Day of Atonement), falling in September-October. After this date, Mediterranean sailing was considered perilous. This is a chronological marker: the voyage was dangerously late in the season.

**Verse 12 -- "Phoenix":** Sinaiticus reads Phoinika. The directional description (kata liba kai kata choron -- "toward southwest and toward northwest") has been debated; the Greek terms lips and choros denote wind directions. I have preserved the literal wind-direction meaning.

**Verse 14 -- "Euraquilo" (Euroakulon):** A hybrid Greek-Latin compound: Euros (Greek, "east wind") + Aquilo (Latin, "north wind"). Sinaiticus reads Euroakulon. This is the reading of the Alexandrian text. The Byzantine tradition reads Eurokludon ("Euroclydon"). The Sinaiticus reading is considered original.

**Verse 16 -- "Cauda":** Sinaiticus reads Kauda. Other manuscripts read Klauda or Klaude. The small island south of Crete is modern Gavdos.

**Verse 17 -- "the Syrtis" (ten Surtin):** The Syrtis Major, treacherous shallows off the North African coast (modern Gulf of Sidra, Libya). "Having lowered the gear" (chalasantes to skeuos) likely refers to a sea-anchor or the main yard, to reduce drift.

**Verse 20 -- "all hope of our being-saved was being taken-away":** The imperfect passive periereito conveys the progressive, grinding loss of hope. I have preserved the periphrastic construction.

**Verse 21 -- "lack-of-appetite" (asitias):** Literally "no-food-ness." This is not a voluntary fast but the inability to eat due to violent seasickness and despair. Sinaiticus reads asitias (genitive).

**Verse 27 -- "the Adriatic" (to Adria):** In antiquity, the Adriatic Sea (Adrias) referred not only to the modern Adriatic but to the broader central Mediterranean between Greece, Italy, and North Africa. The ship was in the sea south of Sicily.

**Verse 28 -- "twenty fathoms... fifteen fathoms":** Greek orguias eikosi... orguias dekapente. An orguia (fathom) is approximately 6 feet (1.8 meters). Twenty fathoms = ~120 feet; fifteen fathoms = ~90 feet. The shoaling bottom confirmed land was near.

**Verse 37 -- "two hundred seventy-six":** Sinaiticus reads diakosioi hebdomekonta hex (276). Some manuscripts (notably Codex Vaticanus corrections and papyrus witnesses) read hos hebdomekonta hex ("about 76"). The Sinaiticus reading of 276 is the majority Alexandrian reading and is retained.

**Verse 38 -- "the wheat" (ton siton):** The cargo was grain. Alexandria-to-Rome grain ships were the largest vessels in the ancient Mediterranean, sometimes carrying several hundred passengers and crew. This explains the large number in v. 37.

**Verse 41 -- "a place between-two-seas" (topon dithalasson):** A sandbar or reef where two currents meet. Traditionally identified as the shoal between Malta and the small island of Salmonetta (now connected to Malta) in St. Paul's Bay.

**Sinaiticus-specific variants of note:**
- v. 14: Euroakulon (not Eurokludon) -- Sinaiticus preserves the Alexandrian reading
- v. 16: Kauda (not Klauda) -- Sinaiticus form
- v. 37: 276 (diakosiai hebdomekonta hex) -- the full number, not "about 76"
- v. 39: Sinaiticus reads exosai (aorist infinitive, "to drive out") where some witnesses read ekbrosai or eksosai
- v. 41: Sinaiticus reads the prow "having stuck-fast remained unmoved" (ereisasa emeinen asaleutos), a vivid eyewitness detail

**Translation philosophy for this chapter:** Acts 27 is one of the most detailed nautical narratives in ancient literature. I have preserved all technical sailing vocabulary as literally as possible. Where Greek nautical terms have no single English equivalent, I have used hyphenated compounds (e.g., "storm-tossed," "ran-aground," "ship-owner") to maintain the single-word force of the Greek. Verb tenses have been carefully maintained: imperfects for ongoing action during the storm, aorists for decisive moments, and the periphrastic constructions that convey the relentless duration of the ordeal.

-- EMET
